source.title: Toxic Chemical Release at the DuPont La Porte Chemical Facility

context.operator: E. I. du Pont de Nemours and Company (DuPont)

actions_taken: Methyl mercaptan storage tank pump turned off at 6:02 am (approximately 3 hours after release began)
Emergency isolation valve activated at 10:07 am (approximately 7 hours after release began)
ERT conducted multiple building entries to search for missing workers
Open drain valves on waste gas vent header closed during second ERT entry
Hot zone established and barricaded at approximately 5:40 am
Harris County Hazardous Materials Team performed off-site air monitoring at 8:07 am
DuPont committed to changes at La Porte facility following CSB findings
DuPont changed LPBC bonus system post-incident to align with OSHA guidance
DuPont added process safety questions to Safety Perception Surveys in 2017
DuPont completed two new baseline PHAs using more robust methodology post-incident
event.summary: On November 15, 2014, approximately 24,000 pounds of highly toxic methyl mercaptan was released from the Lannate insecticide production unit at the DuPont La Porte, Texas chemical facility. The release killed four workers (three operators and a shift supervisor) inside the manufacturing building from asphyxia and acute methyl mercaptan inhalation exposure. The release originated when workers opened drain valves on the waste gas vent header piping, not realising that liquid methyl mercaptan had flowed into the header due to an abnormal valve alignment used during troubleshooting of a hydrate blockage in the methyl mercaptan feed piping. Contributing factors included flawed engineering design of the NRS incinerator vent header, inadequate safeguards against hydrate formation, failed building ventilation fans, an inadequate toxic gas detection and alarm system, normalisation of methyl mercaptan odour and alarms by personnel, absence of management of change for troubleshooting activities, and a severely deficient emergency response. The emergency response was hampered by the loss of the process coordinator (the shift supervisor who was a victim), a non-operational ERT mini-pumper truck, absence of air monitoring, inadequate hot zone control, and underestimation of the release rate. The facility was subsequently closed and dismantled.
recommendations: 2015-01-I-TX-R8: Update DuPont La Porte emergency response plan addressing process coordinator backup, ERT alerting protocols, emergency vehicle maintenance, air monitoring equipment, high-hazard area surveillance, emergency planning document updates, building maps and site tours, process data analysis roles, hot zone training, release characterisation guidance, and fence-line monitoring procedures.
2015-01-I-TX-R9: Local 900C ICWUC/UFCW to work with DuPont to develop and implement the emergency response plan described in R8.
Ensure employees understand hazards of all chemicals they may be exposed to, including methyl mercaptan.
Install automatic audible and visual alarms in manufacturing buildings to alert field personnel of toxic releases.
Preidentify unit experts as technical support personnel with backup capability for emergencies.
Clearly detail alerting and notification protocols for different emergency types in plant procedures.
Develop and apply regular maintenance schedules for emergency response vehicles per NFPA 1911.
Provide ERT with reliable air monitoring equipment and training.
Equip high-hazard areas with detectors, alarms, and surveillance technology.
Develop system to update emergency planning documents when hazards are identified.
Include building maps and floor plans in emergency response plans.
Assign personnel to analyse process data during incidents to identify source and magnitude.
Train ERT to physically designate, communicate, and control hot zone boundaries.
Use conservative public protection zones during releases when accurate release rates are unavailable.
Implement fence-line monitoring for hazardous gases.
Conduct comprehensive assessments of process safety management systems periodically.
Ensure PSM system changes are adopted consistently across all sites.
Invest sufficient time and resources in robust audits.
Assess personal and process safety performance separately.
Ensure PHA teams fully develop hazard scenarios and assign appropriate frequencies and severities.
Ensure corrective actions meet the intent of recommendations before closure.
Develop action plans to identify and control hazards during troubleshooting operations.
Always use MOC where required by corporate policy or federal regulations.
Train personnel on scenarios triggering line-breaking permits and analyse recurring issues.
Ensure effective multi-modal shift communication, especially for novel or complex situations.
Evaluate safety incentive programs to ensure they do not disincentivise injury or incident reporting.

hazards.name: Highly toxic and flammable methyl mercaptan stored and processed under pressure in the Lannate manufacturing unit
Methyl mercaptan hydrate formation potential in feed piping at low temperatures

threats.name: Methyl mercaptan hydrate blockage in feed piping leading to abnormal valve alignment during troubleshooting
Liquid methyl mercaptan ingress into waste gas vent header piping (not designed for liquid)
Manual opening of drain valves on waste gas vent header releasing liquid methyl mercaptan to atmosphere inside manufacturing building
Failed building ventilation fans allowing toxic vapour accumulation
Inadequate toxic gas detection and alarm system failing to warn field workers
Absence of management of change for troubleshooting piping alignment and heating activities
Bypassed safety interlock not restored after turnaround maintenance causing AAO dilution and unit shutdown

consequences.name: Fatal toxic exposure of four workers (asphyxia and acute methyl mercaptan inhalation) inside manufacturing building
Serious toxic exposure injuries to additional workers (Operator 3, Operator 4, Operator 7)
Potential off-site toxic exposure of public from methyl mercaptan plume exceeding ERPG-3 concentrations beyond fence line
Prolonged uncontrolled release of approximately 24,000 pounds of methyl mercaptan over approximately 7 hours
Emergency responders placed at high risk from explosive and toxic atmosphere during building entries
Permanent closure and dismantlement of DuPont La Porte Lannate unit

controls.name: Methyl mercaptan hydrate prevention safeguards (heat tracing on feed piping)
controls.barrier_role: Prevent hydrate formation in methyl mercaptan feed piping at low temperatures
controls.lod_basis: Primary engineering safeguard to prevent hydrate blockage identified in PHA and technical standard

supporting_text: Although the potential for methyl mercaptan hydrate formation had been identified in DuPont's methyl mercaptan technical standard and in process hazard analyses years earlier, DuPont never implemented safeguards, such as heat tracing, or developed a procedure to dissociate the hydrate safely.
DuPont La Porte did not establish adequate safeguards after a 2011 DuPont La Porte process hazard analysis identified hydrate formation in this piping.

controls.name: Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) with adequate safeguard identification for hydrate scenario
controls.barrier_role: Identify and control hazards associated with methyl mercaptan hydrate formation and dissociation
controls.lod_basis: PSM element required to identify hazards and ensure adequate safeguards

supporting_text: The PHA team underestimated the risk, noting, 'Ultimately by heating the line externally it will vaporize and return to [methyl mercaptan] and water vapor.' Even though the PHA team identified heating as a means to dissociate the methyl mercaptan hydrate, DuPont La Porte did not identify the need to develop a formal procedure or engineered system to safely heat the line.
The PHA team assessed the plugging as presenting an unlikely, low-severity hazard, assigning it the lowest possible risk ranking.

controls.name: Management of Change (MOC) for piping alignment and heating activities during troubleshooting
controls.barrier_role: Identify and control hazards introduced by non-standard piping alignments and heating methods used during troubleshooting
controls.lod_basis: PSM and RMP requirement to perform MOC before process changes

supporting_text: DuPont La Porte personnel did not perform an MOC before instructing operations personnel to use the hot water to heat the piping, or before creating the piping alignment.
DuPont corporate standards, as well as the EPA's Risk Management Plan (RMP) rule and OSHA's PSM regulations, require an MOC for these types of process changes.

controls.name: Written operating procedures for vent header draining and line-breaking permits
controls.barrier_role: Ensure safe execution of non-routine drain valve operations on waste gas vent header with appropriate PPE and hazard controls
controls.lod_basis: PSM requirement for safe work practices including line-breaking permits

supporting_text: DuPont La Porte personnel never completed an MOC or established a specific procedure or used safe work practices, such as line-breaking permits, for draining this liquid from the waste gas vent header piping.
DuPont La Porte did not establish a formal procedure for draining liquid from the waste gas vent header piping, aside from its line-breaking policy.
Instructions to workers did not specify that additional precautions—such as wearing additional personal protective equipment (PPE)—were needed when draining the waste gas vent header.

controls.name: Safety interlock program ensuring bypassed interlocks are restored before plant restart
controls.barrier_role: Prevent inadvertent chemical dilution and consequent unit shutdown that initiated the chain of events
controls.lod_basis: PSM mechanical integrity and pre-startup safety review requirements

supporting_text: In the spring of 2014, the chain propagated when DuPont La Porte's interlock program did not require verification that interlocks that had been bypassed for turnaround maintenance were returned to service before the plant resumed operating.

The interlock function had been bypassed in the field so that water could be used to decontaminate the tank during the turnaround in spring 2014. Instrument air supply tubing was connected directly to the interlock valve actuator, bypassing the interlock activation solenoid, and forcing the valve to remain fully open. The interlock could not function because the bypass was not removed at the completion of the turnaround.

controls.name: Manufacturing building ventilation system (rooftop dilution air exhaust fans)
controls.barrier_role: Dilute and remove flammable and toxic vapours from manufacturing building to prevent lethal atmosphere accumulation
controls.lod_basis: Engineering control to maintain safe atmosphere in enclosed building containing toxic chemicals

supporting_text: At the time of the incident, neither of the manufacturing building's two rooftop ventilation fans was working, despite an 'urgent' work order written nearly a month earlier.

One fan had been down since June 2014, five months prior to the incident, due to an electrical problem. On October 20, 2014, the other fan was making a noise significant enough that DuPont operators turned it off and wrote an 'urgent' work order to have it repaired.
Even had the fans worked, they probably would not have prevented a lethal atmosphere inside the building due to the large amount of toxic gas released.

controls.name: Toxic gas detection system with appropriate alarm setpoints inside manufacturing building
controls.barrier_role: Detect methyl mercaptan releases at safe exposure levels and alert field workers to evacuate
controls.lod_basis: Engineering detection barrier to provide early warning of toxic gas release to workers

supporting_text: DuPont La Porte set the detector alarms well above safe exposure limits for workers. The methyl mercaptan detectors alarm at 25 ppm—well above the NIOSH recommended exposure limit (REL) of 0.5 ppm and the OSHA enforceable PEL ceiling limit of 10 ppm.
DuPont La Porte did not provide visual lights or audible alarms for the manufacturing building to warn workers of highly toxic gas concentrations inside it.
DuPont La Porte relied on verbal communication of alarms that automatically displayed on a continuously manned control board.
The building where the workers died was not equipped with an adequate toxic gas detection system to alert personnel to the presence of dangerous chemicals.

controls.name: Personal methyl mercaptan detectors issued to field operators
controls.barrier_role: Provide individual field workers with real-time warning of dangerous methyl mercaptan concentrations
controls.lod_basis: Personal gas detection as a layer of protection for field workers in toxic chemical environments

supporting_text: DuPont La Porte had some personal methyl mercaptan detectors available, but they were never issued to operators to wear in the field. Such detectors could have warned Operator 2, Operator 3, and Operator 4 (personnel who initially responded to Operator 1's distress call) of the high methyl mercaptan concentration inside the manufacturing building.

controls.name: Respiratory protective equipment (SCBA) use during methyl mercaptan alarm response and drain operations
controls.barrier_role: Protect workers from toxic methyl mercaptan inhalation during alarm response and non-routine drain operations
controls.lod_basis: Last line of defence PPE to protect workers from toxic exposure

supporting_text: None of these operators knew of the major release of toxic methyl mercaptan inside of the manufacturing building; therefore, they did not wear any respiratory protection when they ran into the manufacturing building.
DuPont La Porte's instructions did not specify additional breathing protection for this task.
Additionally, when the toxic gas detectors triggered alarms, DuPont La Porte personnel investigated potential methyl mercaptan leaks without using respiratory protection.

controls.name: Emergency isolation valve for methyl mercaptan storage tank
controls.barrier_role: Isolate methyl mercaptan storage tank from process to stop or reduce release rate
controls.lod_basis: Emergency isolation to limit inventory available to release and reduce consequence severity

supporting_text: The methyl mercaptan storage tank pump was not turned off until 6:02 am—three hours after the release began—significantly reducing the rate of the methyl mercaptan release.
The methyl mercaptan storage tank emergency isolation valve was not closed until 10:07 am—seven hours after the release began—finally isolating the methyl mercaptan storage tank from the process.
No one evaluated process data to determine what was causing the release until hours into the response efforts.

controls.name: Emergency Response Team (ERT) with appropriate PPE, air monitoring, and process coordinator support
controls.barrier_role: Respond effectively to toxic chemical release, rescue affected workers, control release, and protect public
controls.lod_basis: Emergency response as mitigation barrier to limit consequences of loss of containment event

supporting_text: The La Porte site's emergency response efforts were characterized by miscommunication, disorganization, and a lack of situational awareness.
The Shift Supervisor, however, was a victim of the toxic chemical release, and DuPont La Porte had not designated an on-site backup process coordinator for this shift.
When ERT members attempted to start the mini-pumper truck that contained SCBAs and radios, however, it would not start and could not make it to the incident scene.
During the ERT's initial entry into the manufacturing building to search for the missing workers, ERT members did not use air monitoring equipment to characterize the building atmosphere.
The ERT did not adequately establish, physically mark, or communicate the boundary of the hot zone during the early stages of the emergency response efforts.

controls.name: Dispersion modelling and fence-line air monitoring for public protection assessment
controls.barrier_role: Assess off-site methyl mercaptan concentrations and trigger public protective actions if required
controls.lod_basis: Emergency response tool to evaluate and communicate public risk during chemical release

supporting_text: The SAFER operator estimated a release rate based upon an ERT member's observations regarding the relative strength of the chemical odor outside of the manufacturing building... 'I just had to plug something in, so I plugged in 10 pounds an hour release rate. … We were just guessing at the release rate.'
DuPont La Porte personnel did not monitor (test) the air for methyl mercaptan along the site's property line (fence line) during the release.

The CSB determined, however, that the emergency response personnel greatly underestimated the release rate... the methyl mercaptan release rate was closer to 9,000 pounds per hour—a rate 900 times greater than the dispersion model input.
Some emergency response personnel believed methyl mercaptan was lighter than air and would not affect the local community.

controls.name: Corrective action program for PSM audit findings (including ventilation system testing)
controls.barrier_role: Ensure identified process safety deficiencies are effectively corrected to prevent incident escalation
controls.lod_basis: PSM compliance audit corrective action program to close safety gaps

supporting_text: In 2009, a second-party PSM audit found that the DuPont La Porte site lacked documentation showing the manufacturing building's ventilation system was ever tested to ensure it could effectively distribute fresh air within the building.
DuPont La Porte closed the corrective action in March 2010 after creating this program. But the CSB found serious deficiencies in the implementation of this program.
DuPont La Porte never verified whether the manufacturing building's ventilation system could effectively remove flammable or toxic chemical leaks.
At times, the corrective action program at DuPont La Porte did not appropriately correct gaps identified by site PSM audits.

controls.name: Shift communication and written handover procedures for abnormal operations
controls.barrier_role: Ensure incoming shift personnel are fully informed of ongoing troubleshooting activities, hazards, and process status
controls.lod_basis: Operational discipline control to maintain situational awareness across shift changes

supporting_text: During the troubleshooting operations, DuPont personnel relied upon verbal communications to convey the Technical Team's plan to the incoming shift.
The only written communication to the night shift supervisor was a note in the shift log that stated: 'Unplugging MeSH Header.'
The Technical Team did not meet with the newly involved night shift supervisor, nor did it communicate verbally or in writing with the night shift operations personnel.

controls.name: Process safety culture assessment program
controls.barrier_role: Identify process safety culture weaknesses and drive corrective actions to prevent systemic PSM failures
controls.lod_basis: Organisational control to identify and address latent process safety weaknesses

supporting_text: DuPont La Porte did not use any type of robust, formal process safety culture assessment.
DuPont La Porte used a proprietary Safety Perception Survey that focused on personal or occupational safety but did not evaluate or assess the process safety culture.

Although DuPont's corporate standard recommended that its sites assess their process safety culture, DuPont La Porte had not formally evaluated process safety culture at its facility before the November 2014 incident.

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
